chunk_summary: This section addresses NICU fortification troubleshooting through scenario-based approaches for safe and effective nutritional management of preterm infants, focusing on weight gain, metabolic bone disease, and feeding tolerance issues.
learning_objectives: Understand the stepwise approach to troubleshooting fortification in NICU settings.
Identify and manage metabolic bone disease risks in predominantly donor human milk-fed infants.
Evaluate and address feeding intolerance in preterm infants transitioning to full-strength fortification.
key_facts: A 1.1 kg VLBW infant with weight gain ~10 g/kg/day should not have multiple modulars added immediately; reassess growth after 3–5 days.
Low serum phosphorus and rising ALP in DHM-fed infants indicate metabolic bone disease risk; confirm vitamin D dosing and increase mineral delivery.
Increased gastric residuals and abdominal distension after full-strength fortification require checks for mixing accuracy, sepsis, or gut pathology.
Avoid prolonged unfortified donor milk exposure to reduce metabolic bone disease risk.
If NEC is suspected, stop feeds and follow the unit NEC evaluation pathway before further nutrition escalation.
reference_quote: Scenario-based troubleshooting supports safe, consistent fortification rather than reactive changes.
document_key_topics: Metabolic bone disease in preterm infants
Feeding intolerance and NEC risk management
